If I could give you one piece of advice it would be
If I could give you one thing from everything I've learned, it would be this:
If a beneficiary has a need before final distribution, I trust you to advance funds reasonably.
If any decision gets hard, make the one you think I would have made. Then let it go.
If beneficiaries disagree — how you want it resolved
If distributions are unequal, the reasoning
If it's a close call and under $10,000, use your judgment. Don't burn months agonizing.
If possible, at home. Not a hospital room.
If someone has left you a message, sign in with the email address it was sent to and you'll find it here, kept safely for you.
If something comes up that I didn't anticipate, do what a fair person would do.
If the end is near and I cannot speak, please read this to me: I love you. I am grateful. I am not afraid. You made my life worth living.
If there is ever ambiguity, default to comfort over aggressive intervention.

My deepest wish is that no one deletes my photos without looking through them first. They took a lifetime.
My dogs are a bonded pair. Please place them together, even if it means they go to someone who isn't my first choice.
My family around me. My favourite music on low. No medical chaos.

My grandmother's ring goes to [name]. Because she was closest to Nana, and because she has her kindness.
My parents [names] are essential in their lives. They must remain central.
My partner [name] knows the plan. Follow their lead for the first year.
My rabbi / priest / imam / minister to be called. Contact in my Treasure Chest.

The most common words executors say are: "I wish I knew what they would have wanted." This section answers that.

The objects that cause the most family conflict are almost never the most valuable ones. They're the ones with meaning — a watch, a ring, a letter, a photo, a chair. This section prevents a lifetime of resentment over a $40 object.
The one you'd record if you only had time for one. The first message your family will hear. No pressure — just the most important thing you want them to know.
The painting in the living room — it meant something to your mother and me. It goes to [name] with the stories behind it, which I've recorded separately.
The person you named is taking on the most important job in the world. Thank them. Reassure them. Tell them you trust them.
The scholarship I fund at [institution] — please continue via [funding source].

Your POA for personal care is the legal document. This section is the human one — the detail behind the instructions. What gives you comfort. What dignity looks like to you. The music, people, and presence you want at the end.

Your executor has the hardest job: administering your estate while grieving you. This section gives them context the will cannot — your intent, your judgment, your permission to make reasonable calls without guilt.
Your family will delete things they shouldn't, and keep things they don't need to, unless you tell them what to do. Five minutes here saves them from that.
